acceptance_targets: [CH06_OPENING_REORIENTATION_DRAG] Pass if, by the material now occupying roughly snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:25, the scene is already doing new dramatic work unique to this chapter: Petra's new cases or the public lockdown consequence is active on the page. Fail if the revised opening still spends its first movement mainly restating that the virus reaches everyone before introducing a new cost, decision, or public consequence.

[CH06_BLACKOUT_CONCEALMENT_LOGIC] Pass if, after the blackout sequence spanning snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:153 through snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:205, a cold reader can answer both "Why didn't Kai summon Petra?" and "Why does Lena keep the card?" from the revised text alone. Fail if the scene still depends on them simply letting a documented episode pass without a legible motive or obstacle.
[RED-015] Ch6 contains at most 2 glasses references. The specific gesture of removing/replacing glasses does not appear more than once.
[CON-003] Every instance of the character's name uses the same spelling (accented or unaccented) from Ch3 through Ch15.
[CON-004] Every reference to Lena's port uses 'base of her skull' or 'base of the skull.' No instance uses 'neck' as the port's location.
[length_chapter_06_min_words] Chapter word count is >= 4000 while preserving heading contract.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, by the material now occupying roughly snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:25, the scene is already doing new dramatic work unique to this chapter: Petra's new cases or the public lockdown consequence is active on the page. Fail if the revised opening still spends its first movement mainly restating that the virus reaches everyone before introducing a new cost, decision, or public consequence.
findings.problem: The opening spends too much of its first movement re-deriving the already-established conclusion that the shared network has compromised everyone, so the chapter enters on reorientation rather than on the new chapter work: fresh cases, public disclosure, and facility-wide consequence. At award level that blunts the aftermath engine instead of turning immediately toward a new cost.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rebuild the opening around fresh consequence at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:17 or snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:65, and compress the repeated baseline setup at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:3, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:11, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:33 into brief orientation. Keep the facts that forty-three athletes are compromised and diagnostics will take days, but stop spending two separate beats re-proving "everyone."

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if, after the blackout sequence spanning snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:153 through snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:205, a cold reader can answer both "Why didn't Kai summon Petra?" and "Why does Lena keep the card?" from the revised text alone. Fail if the scene still depends on them simply letting a documented episode pass without a legible motive or obstacle.
findings.problem: Once Lena visibly loses time in a medical room during a facility-wide reporting emergency, the scene relies on both Kai and Lena treating the episode as private disclosure without enough on-page motive for not calling Petra or filing the symptom card. Because later plot pressure depends on Lena's severity staying partly hidden, this reads as under-motivated concealment rather than earned withholding.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:153, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:157, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:169, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:203, make the non-report choice explicit and costly. Options are fine, but the revised scene must show a legible reason or obstacle: Lena stops Kai from pressing the call button for a stated reason, Kai names a concrete reason to delay, or Lena actively approaches reporting at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_06.md:203 and aborts because of a specific consequence she cannot yet face.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 contains at most 2 glasses references. The specific gesture of removing/replacing glasses does not appear more than once.
findings.evidence: Ch6 contains 5 glasses references across lines 845-954: line 845 'eyes narrowed behind his glasses'; line 895 'glasses had slid down his nose'; line 909 'pushed his glasses back into place'; line 954 'took off his glasses and rubbed the bridge of his nose'. Manuscript total: 22 glasses references (target: 8-10).
findings.problem: Yusuf's glasses appear 5 times in Ch6. The 'slid down / pushed back' and 'took off / rubbed nose' gestures repeat the same nervous-engineer signal. Combined with Ch14's 5 instances, these two chapters account for nearly half of all glasses references in the manuscript.
findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch6, keep 1-2 glasses references — the 'took off and rubbed the bridge of his nose' at line 954 (Petra's arrival, emotional beat) is the strongest. Cut or vary the others so the glasses aren't doing all the characterization work.

findings.acceptance_test: Every instance of the character's name uses the same spelling (accented or unaccented) from Ch3 through Ch15.
findings.evidence: Ch3-4: 'Tomás' (accented) in all 19 instances (Ch3 lines 413-443; Ch4 lines 582-730); Ch6+: 'Tomas' (unaccented) in all 57 instances (Ch6 lines 1148+, Ch8 lines 1430+, Ch14 lines 2800+, Ch15 lines 3049+)
findings.problem: The character's name spelling shifts from 'Tomás' (with accent, Ch3-4) to 'Tomas' (without accent, Ch6 onward). The accent is present in every instance through Ch4 and absent in every instance from Ch6 forward. This is a copyediting inconsistency, not a POV choice — the name changes between chapters that share no common narrator.
findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize the spelling to one form throughout the manuscript. 'Tomás' is the correct Spanish spelling and is used in his introduction chapters. Apply the accent mark consistently across all 76 instances, or drop it consistently across all 76.

findings.acceptance_test: Every reference to Lena's port uses 'base of her skull' or 'base of the skull.' No instance uses 'neck' as the port's location.
findings.evidence: Base-of-skull references: Ch2 line 354, Ch2 line 374, Ch10 line 1814, Ch10 line 1920, Ch11 line 2049, Ch13 line 2577, Ch18 lines 3640 and 3668; Neck references: Ch6 line 1050 'port at her neck', Ch10 line 1880 'port at the back of her neck', Ch15 line 3438 'fingers resting against the small circle of the port' (at back of neck)
findings.problem: Lena's neural link port is described as being at the 'base of her skull' (8 instances) and at 'her neck' or 'the back of her neck' (3+ instances). While anatomically adjacent, the text uses both as if they are the same location without settling on consistent language. The continuity sheet specifies 'base of her skull' as canonical.
findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize to 'base of her skull' or 'base of the skull' in all instances. Change Ch6 line 1050 from 'port at her neck' and Ch10 line 1880 from 'port at the back of her neck' to the canonical location.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter word count is >= 4000 while preserving heading contract.
findings.rewrite_direction: Add causally necessary scene material that strengthens the chapter engine, increases pressure, and lands the planned state shift without filler.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel asks what it means to own a body that has been partially replaced, optimized, or networked. The answer cannot be abstract. The physical horror of enhancement failure — sensory blackouts, autonomous muscle firing, personality thinning — must be rendered with visceral specificity. The intimacy between enhanced people must confront the fact that their bodies are partly engineered, and the strangeness of that must be present on the page, not elided. Margot's addiction to the sensory feed must be shown as a genuine addiction with genuine physical craving, not as an interesting character trait. Tomas's paralysis must be ugly, specific, and permanent — not a tidy plot device. The corporate betrayal must feel institutional, not melodramatic. When the novel asks its readers to sit with discomfort — a body that moves without consent, a mind that thins to nothing, an addiction that destroys someone else — the prose must stay present and not retreat to safety. The specific is always braver than the general.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism at the point of consequence is a craft failure. When a body crashes into a wall at sprint speed, the reader should feel the impact — bone, skin, blood, the specific geometry of injury. When Margot feels Tomas's spinal impact through the feed, the sensation must be rendered in full: the compression, the silence of nerves going dead, the phantom that stays after she disconnects. When Kai's body moves without his consent during sex, the reader must experience the uncanny specificity of reflexes that are not his. When Lena loses time, the gap must feel real — not a graceful ellipsis but a genuine absence. Every scene where the novel touches difficult material — bodily horror, addiction, institutional betrayal, the vulnerability of intimacy — must be rendered with the same specificity given to any other moment. Retreat into summary, metaphor, or tasteful vagueness at these moments destroys the novel's central claim that bodies matter.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters in spoken English. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific moments (Lena's slightly formal second-language register, Petra's clinical precision). Any uncontracted line in casual dialogue should be checked: does this character have a reason to be formal here?

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity chapters with reflective or quiet ones. Vary scene count, dialogue density, and narrative distance across consecutive chapters. A chapter driven by confrontation should not be followed by another confrontation. A chapter heavy in dialogue should be followed by one that relies more on interiority and physical description. The quiet chapters (7, 16, parts of 18) should not feel like connective tissue between the 'real' scenes — they carry the human baseline that makes crisis meaningful. Some chapters should be physically compressed (a single long scene) and others structurally looser (multiple shorter scenes with white space between them).
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary literary — technically precise when inhabiting engineering or medical perspectives, grounded in body experience when inhabiting athletic or intimate perspectives. The enhancement technology should be described through felt sensation, not through exposition. How a neural link feels, not how it works. The novel's primary sensory vocabulary is the body: muscle, bone, nerve, heat, pressure, the experience of a body doing things its owner did not command.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Purple proprioception — the sensory descriptions of enhancement should remain specific and physical, not drift into poetic abstraction about the nature of consciousness
Exposition dumps about how the technology works — establish through character experience, not through narrator explanation
Uniform crisis register — not every chapter should feel urgent; some should feel ordinary, empty, or quiet, and the prose should match
Philosophical narrator — the narration should not editorialize about the body-autonomy theme; the events carry the argument
Symmetrical emotional beats — not every character should have matching epiphanies or matching moments of clarity; some characters end muddled
Mechanical metaphors for human experience — the point is that these people are not machines, even when their bodies partly are; do not let the prose treat them as systems
Identical blackout descriptions — each sensory disruption scene must find different language for the experience rather than reusing void/static/white metaphors
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating POV by chapter. The narration inhabits each POV character's sensory and cognitive world — what they notice, how they process sensation, what language they would use internally. The narration should not be smarter, more articulate, or more philosophically aware than the character whose head we are in. Ensemble chapters may shift between characters within scenes but must establish clear transitions.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable across chapters and within chapters. Technical/calibration scenes: precise, measured, slightly clinical. Sensory/feed scenes: fractured, overlapping, synaesthetic. Interpersonal scenes: looser, more conversational, allowing silence and dead space. Action/blackout scenes: compressed, fragmented, physical. Quiet scenes: unhurried, sensory, present tense of ordinary experience. No single rhythm should dominate the novel.
